extra.reasoning:
Decision system with inputs diabetes, prior_gi_bleed and per-output priority conflict resolution (highest priority wins; ties by order). The property requires that (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b) is never jointly true.

Analysis of rec_drug_b: When diabetes is true, two rules target rec_drug_b: guard_suppress_b (priority 90, sets false) and rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50, sets true). Priority 90 > 50, so guard_suppress_b wins and rec_drug_b := false. When diabetes is false, no rule sets rec_drug_b, so the default applies and rec_drug_b := false. Therefore rec_drug_b is false for every input.

Since rec_drug_b is always false, (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b) is always false regardless of rec_drug_a.

Full enumeration of the 4 input combinations:
1. diabetes=F, gi_bleed=F: a=false(default), b=false(default) -> a&b=false.
2. diabetes=F, gi_bleed=T: a=false, b=false -> a&b=false.
3. diabetes=T, gi_bleed=F: a=true(rec_a_from_conjunction), b=false(guard p90 over trigger p50) -> a&b=false.
4. diabetes=T, gi_bleed=T: a=true, b=false -> a&b=false.

In all cases the conjunction is false. The priority-90 guard suppressing rec_drug_b is precisely what guarantees mutual exclusion. The property holds over the entire input space; no counterexample exists.
